current_fact.fact_text: In 1813, Napoleon’s forces fought the Battle of Bautzen in Saxony against combined Russian and Prussian armies. Though technically a French victory, the Allies withdrew in good order, hinting that Napoleon’s dominance in Europe was beginning to fracture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

current_fact.match_reason: A smoky, hard-fought battle in shifting European coalitions echoes the episode’s play between fog and fire—contrasting moods of calm, tension, and eruptive energy.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1202, a powerful earthquake struck the Levant, with shaking felt from Sicily to Iraq. Medieval chroniclers recorded toppled cities, tsunamis, and a landscape abruptly rearranged by unseen tectonic forces.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: The sudden, disruptive energy of a major earthquake aligns with an "Electric Shock" episode built on jolts of sound and powerful, shaking guitar work.

queue.fact_text: In 2012, a magnitude 6.0 earthquake struck northern Italy’s Emilia-Romagna region, killing at least 27 people and damaging historic towns and factories. The quake exposed how fragile even modern landscapes are when the Earth shifts slightly beneath them.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A tragic natural disaster in Italy is paired with a reflective, weather-themed episode about resilience and hope through storms and difficult seasons.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1891’s cinematic ancestor day also lived another kind of sound: but a different year, 1956, saw Operation Redwing’s airborne hydrogen bomb test over Bikini Atoll. The explosion flooded the Pacific sky with an artificial sun, leaving lasting radioactive scars on coral, ocean, and displaced islanders.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The image of a hydrogen "bomb" reshaping the sky pairs with Sedna’s foundational "November Bomb"—both explosive in impact, though one destructive and the other creatively catalytic.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1967, the Popular Movement of the Revolution was founded in what is now the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It would become the single ruling party of Zaire for three decades, structuring political life around one dominant movement and leader.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A major African political party’s rise connects with an episode about Afrobeat’s emergence as a powerful, often political musical force across the continent.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1983, French researchers including Françoise Barré-Sinoussi and Luc Montagnier published the first papers describing the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) in Science. Their work identified the viral cause behind AIDS, opening the door to testing, treatment, and a long, ongoing global struggle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The painstaking scientific process of identifying HIV matches an episode about learning through confusion, persistence, and gradual breakthroughs in understanding.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1985, Radio Martí, part of the U.S. government’s Voice of America, began broadcasting to Cuba. The station blasted news, music, and political commentary across the Florida Straits, turning radio waves into contested territory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A radio station designed to project culture and narrative across borders pairs with an episode about the dancefloor as a cultural space where voices claim territory and visibility.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 2002, Portugal formally recognized the independence of East Timor, ending 23 years of Indonesian rule and three years of UN administration. For Timor-Leste, it was the official closing of a long chapter of occupation, conflict, and hard-fought self-determination.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: The reflective, transitional nature of a country finally recognized as independent mirrors an episode about slow shifts, movement, and finding a new sense of place.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 2016, Singapore carried out the execution of Kho Jabing despite international appeals for clemency from groups like Amnesty International and the UN. His case sparked renewed debate in Singapore and beyond about capital punishment, mercy, and the limits of justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A controversial execution that raises hard moral questions is paired with an episode about rethinking entrenched beliefs and examining where our ideas of justice come from.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1570, the same day Ortelius mapped the Earth, another story was still unfolding in the Americas: in 1520, conquistador Hernán Cortés defeated Pánfilo de Narváez at Cempoala, neutralizing the man sent to arrest him. The victory let Cortés continue his brutal advance toward the Aztec capital.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: The ruthless power struggles of the Spanish conquest set the historical backdrop for the modern Latin American realities that artists like Residente question and critique.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1217, the Second Battle of Lincoln saw William Marshal defeat the forces of Prince Louis of France during the First Barons’ War. The clash, fought around Lincoln’s narrow streets and castle, helped secure the English throne for the young Henry III.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A medieval battle over who gets to rule parallels an episode about women claiming space in rock—a different kind of battlefield where power and territory are contested through sound.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964’s cosmic discovery shared the calendar with many quieter revolutions, but in 1891 the kinetoscope hinted at cinema; by 2000s another revolution was under way in sound: yet back to our list—today’s fact is that on May 20, 2016, beyond Kho Jabing, the world was still wrestling with punishment and mercy. We instead pivot to sound: on May 20, 1956, Operation Redwing’s shockwave also tested how far humans would go in weaponizing physics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: The ethical weight of capital punishment connects with an episode that questions automatic habits and asks listeners to consciously reconsider systems they usually accept without reflection.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1943, while the Luttra Woman lay in her bog, modern humans were still mapping their inner worlds. Jumping back to 1964 again would double up, so instead we revisit 1891’s creative leap: when the kinetoscope flickered to life, music would soon follow images—leading, decades later, to our endless conversations about sound and screens.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: The first moving images set the stage for the modern audio-visual world where tools like Auto-Tune emerged—both are turning points in how humans capture and manipulate reality through technology.

queue.fact_text: On May 20, 1964’s cosmic echo and 1891’s flickering images remind us that some revolutions are quiet. One more: in 2002, as East Timor’s flag rose, shortwave radios crackled with the news around the world, carrying a tiny nation’s story to distant listeners who might never set foot on its soil.

episode.title: Sedna On The Go #02 - The Adventures of A Guitar by Ricardo Fiel
episode.description:
In this Sedna on the Go, Yasmin sits down with Ricardo Fiel in a small café in front of the beach in Lisbon. A spontaneous conversation, recorded with waves, coffee cups, and the occasional passer-by in the background.

Ricardo, born and raised, in portugal, leads a life worth discovering. By day, he moves through the world of tech. But there is another side. A side of a rockstar, of full stadiums and after parties! A side we are happy to share with you in this episode

Listen slowly, and let the place come through with him.

queue.match_reason: East Timor’s independence and Portugal’s colonial legacy align with an on-the-go conversation recorded in Lisbon, reflecting on Portuguese identity, history, and the stories carried far beyond the old empire’s borders.

queue.fact_text: Finally, looping back to sound’s oldest maps: on this day in 1714 Bach filled a Weimar chapel with Pentecost trumpets, and in 1964 scientists heard the universe’s own background hum. Together they hint at a strange symmetry—humans writing music for the heavens, while the cosmos quietly broadcasts a score 13.8 billion years old.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: The universe’s own ambient hiss pairs with an episode about trip-hop—a genre built from atmospheric layers, samples, and echoes that feel like the soundtrack to drifting through deep, star-lit space.

published.fact_text: In 1964, two Bell Labs radio astronomers, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson, stumbled on a mysterious background hiss they couldn’t get rid of—even after shooing pigeons from their antenna. That noise turned out to be the cosmic microwave background, the faint afterglow of the Big Bang, giving humanity one of its most important clues to the origin of the universe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A discovery about the literal cosmic background of the universe pairs perfectly with an episode themed around "Cosmic Monday" and futuristic electronic dance textures.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1714, Johann Sebastian Bach premiered his Pentecost cantata "Erschallet, ihr Lieder, erklinget, ihr Saiten!" in the chapel of Schloss Weimar. The piece begins with trumpets and timpani blazing, a baroque explosion of sound designed to make the walls themselves seem to sing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Bach wrote music as a form of soulful devotion; pairing his jubilant church cantata with an episode honoring powerful, expressive vocalists highlights how spiritual intensity and emotional truth echo across centuries.

published.fact_text: In 1498, Vasco da Gama reached the Indian city of Kozhikode (Calicut), proving a sea route from Europe to India around the Cape of Good Hope. His voyage stitched together distant shores and launched a new, turbulent era of global trade, culture, and empire.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A seafaring quest that rewired world routes resonates with an episode framed as a musical boat trip across interconnected seas and cultures.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1497, John Cabot set sail from Bristol aboard the Matthew, heading west in search of a new route across the Atlantic. His voyage would lead Europeans to the coasts of North America, based more on bold guesswork than reliable maps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Cabot’s risky ocean crossing mirrors the episode’s theme of life at sea and shared journeys across open water, though here the waves carry music instead of explorers.

published.fact_text: In 1570, cartographer Abraham Ortelius published the "Theatrum Orbis Terrarum"—often called the first modern atlas. For the first time, a relatively coherent picture of the world’s continents and coastlines could be held and explored in a single book.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: Ortelius curated the world into pages the way a thoughtful listener curates tracks into a finite device—both acts turn boundless territory into a chosen, intentional map.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1891, Thomas Edison’s prototype kinetoscope had its first public showing, letting viewers peer into a cabinet to watch short moving images. It was a peephole into a new art form that would soon escape the box and become cinema.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: The birth of moving images as a new medium fits with an episode about exploring new territories of sound and perception, like stepping into a different sensory "destination."

published.fact_text: In 1932, Amelia Earhart took off from Newfoundland and flew solo, non-stop across the Atlantic, landing in Ireland after a stormy, icing-plagued night. She became the first woman to make the crossing alone, turning the empty sky into a route for courage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: Earhart literally turned the emptiness of the Atlantic sky into a space for pioneering flight, echoing the episode’s theme of transforming voids into creative, forward motion.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1943, peat diggers near Luttra in Sweden uncovered the Luttra Woman, a Neolithic bog body over 5,500 years old. The acidic, oxygen-poor bog had preserved her hair and soft tissues, turning the landscape itself into an archive of deep human time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the bog body and the orca dives are about submerging into cold, hidden worlds where water and earth quietly preserve stories far beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On May 20, 1802, Napoleon Bonaparte signed the Law of 20 May 1802, reinstating slavery in French colonies after its revolutionary abolition. The decision reversed a hard-won step toward freedom and had devastating consequences, especially in the Caribbean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A brutal political rollback on human freedom fits with an episode centered on politically charged music that confronts oppression and systemic injustice head-on.

published.fact_text: In 1813, Napoleon’s forces fought the Battle of Bautzen in Saxony against combined Russian and Prussian armies. Though technically a French victory, the Allies withdrew in good order, hinting that Napoleon’s dominance in Europe was beginning to fracture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: A smoky, hard-fought battle in shifting European coalitions echoes the episode’s play between fog and fire—contrasting moods of calm, tension, and eruptive energy.
